major_assignments.title: Object-Oriented Library System
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a library management system using object-oriented programming principles. The system should model books (with different types: physical, ebook, audiobook), patrons (students and faculty with different borrowing privileges), and loans with due dates. Implement a class hierarchy with proper inheritance, polymorphism through a common interface, and robust exception handling with custom exceptions for domain errors like borrowing an unavailable book or exceeding loan limits. Include a comprehensive pytest test suite that validates all major operations and error conditions.
objectives: Design a class hierarchy modeling real-world entities and their relationships using inheritance and composition
Apply encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism in a practical multi-class system
Implement custom exception classes for domain-specific error conditions
Write a comprehensive pytest test suite validating all major functionality and error paths
Use abstract base classes to define common interfaces for book and patron types
deliverables: Python package with library system implementation organized into modules (models/, services/, exceptions/)
pytest test suite with at least 25 test cases covering all major operations and error conditions
UML class diagram showing all classes, their relationships, and key methods
Design document (2-3 pages) explaining architecture decisions, class hierarchy rationale, and trade-offs considered
Demo script that exercises all major features: add books, register patrons, checkout, return, search, overdue detection

criteria: Class hierarchy properly models the domain with at least 6 classes (Book types, Patron types, Loan, Library)
Inheritance is used meaningfully with method overriding for different book/patron behaviors
Custom exceptions (BookNotAvailableError, LoanLimitExceededError, etc.) provide clear error handling
Checkout/return operations correctly manage availability state, due dates, and patron records
Encapsulation prevents invalid state transitions (cannot return a book you did not borrow)
Polymorphism enables flexible handling of different book and patron types
Properties or methods enforce invariants (loan limits, valid dates, non-negative quantities)
Code is organized into logical modules with clear separation of concerns
At least 25 test cases covering normal operations, edge cases, and error conditions
Tests verify exception types and messages, not just that exceptions are raised
Tests are well-organized with descriptive names following test_action_condition_expected pattern
All tests pass with no warnings
UML diagram accurately reflects the implementation and is readable
Design document explains key architecture decisions with alternatives considered
Code has docstrings for all classes and public methods
Demo script shows a complete workflow from empty library to active loans
Well-designed class hierarchy demonstrating mastery of OOP principles
Complete library operations with robust custom exception handling
Comprehensive test suite with 25+ tests validating all major functionality
Professional documentation including UML diagram and design rationale

major_assignments.title: Data Structures Implementation and Analysis
major_assignments.description: Implement three fundamental data structures from scratch — a linked list, a stack, and a queue — each as a proper Python class with complete functionality. Then implement two sorting algorithms (insertion sort and merge sort) and two searching algorithms (linear and binary search). Benchmark all implementations on varying input sizes and write an analysis report comparing their theoretical and empirical performance. This project bridges the gap between using Python's built-in data structures and understanding how they work internally.
objectives: Implement linked list, stack, and queue data structures with full ADT operations
Implement sorting and searching algorithms with correct edge case handling
Benchmark implementations empirically with varying input sizes and input patterns
Analyze time complexity theoretically and compare with empirical measurements
Write clean, well-documented implementations suitable for a data structures textbook
deliverables: Python modules: linked_list.py, stack.py, queue.py, sorting.py, searching.py with complete implementations
pytest test suite with at least 30 test cases across all data structures and algorithms
Benchmarking script that produces timing data for varying input sizes (100, 1000, 10000, 100000)
Analysis report (3-4 pages) with Big-O analysis, benchmark results (tables or charts), and comparison discussion
README with instructions for running tests and benchmarks

criteria: Linked list supports append, prepend, insert, delete, search, reverse, and __len__/__str__
Stack and queue implement full ADT with proper encapsulation (no direct internal access)
Sorting algorithms produce correct results on empty, single-element, already-sorted, and reverse-sorted inputs
Binary search handles all edge cases: empty list, single element, element not present, duplicate elements
Each data structure is a clean class with docstrings and consistent method naming
Implementation code is separated from benchmarking and testing code
Common patterns (Node class, comparison logic) are factored into reusable components
Code follows PEP 8 style consistently
Big-O analysis is correct for all operations of each data structure
Benchmarks use appropriate methodology (multiple runs, averaged, random/sorted/reverse inputs)
Empirical results align with theoretical predictions
Report discusses any discrepancies between theory and practice with plausible explanations
Analysis report is well-structured with clear tables or charts
Big-O derivations show reasoning, not just final answers

Correct implementations of all data structures with full ADT operations
Correct sorting and searching algorithms handling all edge cases
Rigorous benchmarking with empirical results supporting theoretical analysis
Comprehensive test suite and clear analysis report

major_assignments.title: GUI Application with Persistent Storage
major_assignments.description: Build a complete desktop application using Tkinter that manages persistent data through JSON serialization. Choose from: a personal finance tracker (income, expenses, categories, budgets, reports), a recipe manager (recipes, ingredients, search, meal planning), or a student grade tracker (courses, assignments, weights, GPA calculation). The application must have a multi-screen GUI with navigation, data persistence between sessions, input validation with error dialogs, and summary statistics or reports. Apply all OOP, error handling, and design principles from the course.
objectives: Design and implement a multi-screen Tkinter application with professional layout and navigation
Apply MVC (Model-View-Controller) or similar separation between GUI, logic, and data layers
Implement JSON-based data persistence with proper error handling for file operations
Create an intuitive user experience with input validation, error dialogs, and informative feedback
Produce a polished application that could be used by a non-technical person

At least 4 distinct screens/views with navigation between them
JSON data file created on first run, loaded on subsequent runs, saved automatically on changes

Design document (2 pages) explaining architecture, class responsibilities, and data model

criteria: Application has at least 4 functional screens with complete CRUD operations for the domain

major_assignments.title: Cross-Language Capstone: Python and Java
major_assignments.description: Implement a non-trivial program in both Python and Java, then write a comparative analysis. Choose from: a text-based game (Python terminal + Java console), a data processor (CSV analysis in both languages), or a collection manager (library/inventory/contacts with OOP in both). The implementations should be functionally equivalent and demonstrate OOP principles, error handling, and appropriate data structures in each language. The comparative report analyzes the design differences, trade-offs, and your experience as a developer working in both languages.
objectives: Implement the same application in two programming languages, demonstrating fluency in both
Apply OOP design principles (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism) in both Python and Java
Compare type systems, error handling approaches, and collection frameworks across languages
Write a thoughtful comparative analysis based on first-hand implementation experience
Demonstrate the ability to learn and apply a new programming language (Java) independently
deliverables: Complete Python implementation with OOP, error handling, and appropriate data structures
Complete Java implementation with equivalent functionality using Java OOP and collections
Comparative analysis report (3-4 pages) covering language differences, design trade-offs, and developer experience
Test demonstrations for both implementations showing equivalent behavior
5-minute presentation comparing the two implementations with code examples

criteria: Python implementation uses OOP with inheritance, custom exceptions, and appropriate data structures

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged, but all code must be written independently. Pair programming is allowed on labs only when explicitly assigned.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Use of AI code generators must be disclosed. Submitting code written by another person or AI without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python 3.12+, Java JDK 17+, VS Code (with Python and Java extensions), and pytest installed. Git familiarity is expected. Lab computers are available but personal machines are recommended.
